title: Two border stars — locking them into one calm light
prompt: Taking your steer literally: keep BOTH, lock them so they move as ONE calm light at the same speed (the guide pulse leads, the pretty edge-glow follows), and let the nav go quiet when the pulse is off on the cards — with "drop the pulse later" still on the table. Here are 5 ways that lock can work, from tightest fusion to a looser handoff. All are calm and all go dark when idle; they differ in HOW the two couple. The edge-glow's look (Orchid → pink bright conic body) is preserved in every one. Pick the ones worth pursuing, note what you'd tune, and I'll narrow toward the winner. Dials are pre-set to where your answers landed — nudge any and I'll re-tune.

options.label: Lockstep Lead-Follow
options.description: Both survive on ONE shared JS clock at the SAME speed. The guide-pulse head leads; the conic edge-glow rides a fixed short gap behind it around the same nav-border path — one calm elongated light. Nav dark when the pulse is off on the cards. Survives: A + B (re-clocked). Clock: guidePath geometry drives both.

options.rationale: Your 'keep both locked together, moving at the same speed, pulse leading' — drawn literally: one shared clock, a fixed small gap between the leading pulse and the trailing edge-glow.

options.label: Merge Into One
options.description: No visible gap — the two collapse into a SINGLE calm light: the edge-glow's conic body carrying the pulse's bright leading node, one object doing both jobs. The cleanest read of 'one unified light,' and the shortest path to eventually dropping the separate pulse. Survives: one merged light. Clock: single source.

options.rationale: Your top survey pick was 'One unified light' — this fuses the two with no visible gap: a single calm light that is the edge-glow's body plus the pulse's bright head.

options.label: Keep the Glow, Drop the Pulse
options.description: The future fallback you named, shown today: remove the guide pulse, keep ONLY the pretty conic edge-glow gliding the nav border, calm and slow, dark when idle. Least engineering — but loses the wayfinding function. Survives: B only. Clock: existing CSS, calmed.

options.rationale: You said the edge-glow is your preference and you 'may ultimately remove the wayfinder pulse' — here's that endpoint drawn now: keep only the pretty glow.

options.label: Calm Afterglow
options.description: The pulse leads and DEPOSITS a slow-fading glow along the border it traces; the nav lights as it passes, then decays to dark by itself (your 'go quiet when away,' by construction). The trail wears the edge-glow's look. Survives: A + a decay buffer. Clock: rAF pulse + per-edge fade.

options.rationale: You wanted the nav to 'go dark / quiet' when the pulse is away — here that's causal: the pulse leaves a slow-fading glow that decays to dark on its own until the next lap.

options.label: Gentle Handoff
options.description: The looser foil: not rigidly glued. The pulse arrives at the nav, the edge-glow gently takes the light and holds a slow brief glide, then quiets to dark when the pulse departs. Feels organic rather than mechanical. Survives: A + B, softly coupled. Clock: event-triggered, not frame-locked.

survey.elaborationPrompt: Elaborate — what direction should the next round take?
